1: Shomer raglekha ka'asher telekh el-beit ha'Elohim — 'Guard your steps when you go to the house of God.' The verb shamar (to guard/keep) carries covenant weight — it is the word used for keeping the commands (Exodus 20:6) and guarding the garden (Genesis 2:15). The feet represent one's approach and posture. Qarov lishmo'a tov mi-teit ha-kesil zebakh — 'to draw near to listen is better than to offer the sacrifice of fools.' The comparison inverts the expected liturgical priority: attentive hearing precedes offering. Ki einam yod'im la'asot ra' — 'who do not realize they are doing wrong' — literally 'who do not know to do evil,' an ironic formula: the fool's sacrifice is evil precisely because it proceeds without hearing.
2: Al temaher al-pikha — 'Do not be hasty with your words.' The verb bahal (H926, to be hasty/dismayed/terrified) is a strong word: it suggests being swept away by impulse. Ve-libekha al yemaher lehozi dabar lifnei ha'Elohim — 'do not be quick in your heart to speak before God.' The heart (lev) is the organ of intention and deliberation; the Teacher's concern is with the inner disposition that produces hasty speech, not just the speech itself. Ki ha'Elohim bashamayim ve'atah al-ha'aretz — 'for God is in heaven and you are on earth' — the spatial asymmetry establishes the asymmetry of the relationship. Al-ken yih'yu devarekha me'atim — 'so let your words be few' — restraint as the appropriate creaturely response to the Creator's transcendence.
3: Ki ba hachalot be-rov inyan — 'For a dream comes from too many concerns.' Rov inyan (too many concerns/business) — the word inyan (H6045, business/preoccupation) recurs from Ecclesiastes 1:13; here it generates the unsettled sleep that produces dreams. Ve-qol ha-kesil be-rov devarim — 'and a fool's chatter from too many words.' The parallel structure is precise: as many concerns → dream, so many words → foolish speech. Both are signs of a mind not at rest. The implication of the comparison: the fool's excessive speech is the waking equivalent of meaningless dreaming — activity without content.
4: Ka'asher tidar neder la'Elohim al-te'acher leshalemo — 'When you make a vow to God, do not delay in fulfilling it.' The noun neder (H5088, vow) is a solemn promise made to God, typically conditional ('if you do X, I will do Y' — cf. Genesis 28:20-22; Numbers 30:2; Deuteronomy 23:21-23). The specific prohibition against delay picks up Deuteronomy 23:21: 'If you make a vow to YHWH your God, do not delay fulfilling it.' Ki ein chafetz ba-kesiylim — 'for he takes no pleasure in fools.' The verb chafatz (to delight/take pleasure in) is the covenant word for God's delight in his people (Psalms 18:19; Isaiah 62:4). Its negation here is severe: the fool who makes and fails to keep a vow has forfeited the possibility of divine delight.
5: Tov asher lo-tidor mishtidar ve-lo teshalem — 'It is better not to vow than to vow and not fulfill it.' The tov min (better than) comparative form appears frequently in Ecclesiastes 7; here it appears in the vow instruction as the pragmatic conclusion: the lesser obligation (no vow) is preferable to the greater broken obligation. Deuteronomy 23:22 states explicitly: 'If you refrain from vowing, it will not be sin to you' — the Teacher cites this practical wisdom directly. The structure of the statement is a Solomonic legal clarification: it does not encourage vow-making, but sets the threshold for integrity when a vow is made.
6: Al-titein et-pikha lachati et-besarekha — 'Do not let your mouth lead your flesh into sin.' The unusual phrase lachati et-besarekha (to make your flesh sin) suggests that the consequences of rash vow-making reach beyond the speaker to their whole person. Ve-al-tomar lifnei ha-malakh ki shegagah hi — 'and do not tell the priest it was a mistake.' The word malakh (messenger/angel) here refers to the cultic priest (cf. Malachi 2:7: the priest as God's messenger). The post-hoc rationalization ('I didn't mean it') is the specific danger the Teacher addresses. Lamah yiqtzof ha'Elohim al qolekha — 'Why should God be angry at your words?' The verb qatsaf (to be angry/wrathful) describes the serious divine response to vow-breaking (Numbers 30:2; Deuteronomy 23:21).
7: Ki be-rov chalomot vahalim u-devarim harbeh — 'When there are many dreams, there are futile words aplenty.' Havalim is the plural of hevel: the connection is explicit — many dreams, many words, are manifestations of the book's central category of futility. The instruction ends with two Hebrew words: ki et-ha'Elohim yera — 'fear God.' This two-word conclusion is the compressed positive instruction: after the extended warning about vows and rash words, the positive command is the simplest possible — reverential awe before God. The yir'at Elohim (fear of God) is the foundation of wisdom (Proverbs 1:7) and the repeated counsel throughout Ecclesiastes (Ecclesiastes 3:14; Ecclesiastes 5:7; Ecclesiastes 7:18; Ecclesiastes 8:12-13; Ecclesiastes 12:13).
8: Im chamas rash ve-gezel mishpat va-tzedek tir'eh ba-medinah — 'If you see the poor being oppressed and justice and equity denied in a district.' Chamas (H2555, violence/oppression) and gezel (H1500, robbery/extortion) describe two forms of social wrongdoing; mishpat va-tzedek (justice and righteousness) are the pillars of YHWH's throne (Psalms 89:14) now denied to the poor. Al-titamah al-ha-chefetz — 'do not be surprised at the matter' — tamah (to be amazed/astonished). The explanation follows: ki gavohah me'al gavohah shomer — 'for one official is covered by a higher one' — a bureaucratic hierarchy where each level provides cover for the level below. The Teacher is describing systemic corruption, not individual evil.
9: Ve-yitron eretz ba-kol hi melekh le-sadeh ne'evad — 'Yet in all this, there is an advantage that belongs to everyone: even a king is served by the cultivated land.' This verse is one of the most contested in Ecclesiastes — the Hebrew is compressed and the interpretations numerous. The MKT reads it as a grounding observation: despite bureaucratic oppression (Ecclesiastes 5:8), the land itself remains the source of sustenance for all, including the king. The agricultural basis of all wealth means that even the highest level of the corrupt hierarchy depends on the same soil that serves the lowest. The yitron here (advantage) is not the ultimate yitron the Teacher has been seeking, but a practical sufficiency — the earth provides for all.
10: Ohev kesef lo-yisba kesef — 'Whoever loves money is never satisfied with money.' Ohev (H157, lover/one who loves) — the same root as the covenant love word ahavah; here it describes disordered attachment. The insatiability of the money-lover connects back to the sea that is 'never full' (Ecclesiastes 1:7) and the eye 'not satisfied' (Ecclesiastes 1:8): the same structure of endless appetite that defines the under-the-sun condition. Mi-ohev be-hamon lo-tevu'ah — 'whoever loves wealth is never satisfied with income' — hamon (abundance/crowd/wealth) and tevu'ah (income/produce): wealth in quantity and wealth in flow both fail to satisfy. Gam-zeh havel — 'this too is vapor' — the brief verdict.
11: Be-robh hatovah rabhu okheleyha — 'As goods increase, so do those who consume them.' The accumulation of goods attracts its own set of consumers — servants, dependents, family, hangers-on — so that the net benefit to the owner diminishes as the gross wealth increases. U-mah-kisron le-vaalah ki im-re'iyat einav — 'what benefit remains for the owner beyond seeing them with his own eyes?' The only genuine benefit of accumulated wealth is the visual experience of possessing it. Kisron (H3666, advantage/profit) — related to kishron (skill) and yitron; a word unique to Ecclesiastes. The bitter irony: the owner's only gain from growing wealth is looking at it.
12: Metukah shnat ha'aved im-me'at ve-im-harbeh yokhal — 'The sleep of the laborer is sweet, whether he eats little or much.' The word metukah (H4966, sweet) is sensory — the sleep is genuinely enjoyable, not merely the absence of insomnia. Ve-hasava le'ashir einennu meniyach lo lishon — 'but the excess of wealth allows the rich man no sleep.' The word sava (H7647, satiety/excess) connects back to the insatiable eye and ear (Ecclesiastes 1:8) and the sea (Ecclesiastes 1:7): too much wealth creates the same structural insatiability. The social irony: the laborer who has little sleeps sweetly; the wealthy man who has too much cannot sleep. Abundance is a burden, not a rest.
13: Yesh ra'ah cholah ra'iti tahat hashemesh — 'Here is a grievous evil I have observed under the sun.' The adjective cholah (H2470, sick/grievous) intensifies ra'ah (evil) — this is not merely bad but sickening evil. The phrase ra'ah cholah is a Ecclesiastes formula for the worst category of bad outcome (also Ecclesiastes 5:16; Ecclesiastes 6:2). Osher shamur li-v'alav le-ra'ato — 'wealth hoarded by its owner at his own expense' — the prepositional phrase le-ra'ato ('to his own harm/evil') is the paradox: the hoarded wealth designed to benefit the owner destroys him instead.
14: Ve-abad ha-osher hahu be-inyan ra' — 'Those riches were lost in a bad venture.' Inyan ra' (H6045 + H7451, bad business/evil affair) — the same inyan word used for the 'unhappy business' God gives to humanity (Ecclesiastes 1:13): bad business can describe both God's assignment of existence and the specific bad investment that loses the wealth. Ve-holid ben ve-ein be-yado me'umah — 'and he had fathered a son, but had nothing to leave him.' The word ben (son) triggers the inheritance problem of Ecclesiastes 2:18-21: a son exists but has nothing to inherit. The wealthy man's failure leaves his son as destitute as the stillborn child who receives no inheritance.
15: Ka'asher yatza mi-beten immo arumm yashuv laleshet ke-shehar ba — 'As he came from his mother's womb, so he will leave — naked.' The wordplay: arumm (naked) echoes the nakedness of Adam and Eve (Genesis 2:25; Ecclesiastes 3:7, 10-11) — the human's fundamental condition before the fall and at death. U-me'umah lo-yisa ba'amalo — 'he can take nothing for his labor that he could carry in his hand.' Amal here in both senses: the activity and its fruit. The man's hands — the organs of his labor — go empty into death. The connection to Job 1:21 ('naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked I shall return') places this observation in a long wisdom tradition about death's radical dispossession.
16: Ve-gam-zot ra'ah cholah — 'This too is a grievous evil.' The formula reinforces the category: the naked departure (Ecclesiastes 5:15) is evaluated with the same ra'ah cholah judgment as the hoarded wealth. Ke-khol-shevar ken yelekh — 'just as he came, so he goes' — the exact parallel of birth-departure. U-mah-yitron lo she-yamal la-ruach — 'what does anyone gain who labors for the wind?' Yitron reappears: the accumulator who goes naked into death has achieved zero net advantage — all the labor has been for wind (la-ruach), the same word as in 'chasing the wind' but now as the destination of labor rather than its futile pursuit.
17: Gam kol-yamav ba-choshekh yokhal — 'Throughout his days he eats in darkness.' Ba-choshekh (in darkness) — not literal night but the metaphorical darkness of joylessness and anxiety. The laborer of Ecclesiastes 5:12 sleeps sweetly; the rich man of Ecclesiastes 5:17 eats in darkness. Ve-cha'as harbeh ve-choli ve-qa'as — 'burdened with much sickness, vexation, and anger.' Ka'as (H3708, vexation/frustration) appears at 1:18 for the sorrow that accompanies increased knowledge; here it describes the existential condition of the anxiety-ridden accumulator. The three-fold burden (sickness, vexation, anger) is the experiential reality behind the ra'ah cholah verdict.
18: Hineh asher ra'iti ani tov asher yafeh le'echol ve-lishtot — 'This is what I have found to be good and fitting.' The word yafeh (H3303, beautiful/fitting/appropriate) — the same root as 'he has made everything beautiful in its time' (Ecclesiastes 3:11). The carpe diem positive counsel is framed with the same aesthetic category as God's creative ordering: the appropriate enjoyment of food, drink, and labor is beautiful — a fit response to existence. Ki hu-helqo — 'for that is one's portion' — helq reappears (cf. Ecclesiastes 2:10; Ecclesiastes 3:22): the portion that genuinely belongs to the person. The alternative of anxious hoarding destroys the capacity for the helq that is actually available.
19: Gam kol-ha'adam asher natan-lo ha'Elohim osher u-nekhasim — 'Furthermore, when God gives someone wealth and possessions.' The distinction between Ecclesiastes 5:13-17 and Ecclesiastes 5:19 is crucial: Ecclesiastes 5:13's osher shamur is wealth hoarded; Ecclesiastes 5:19's osher u-nekhasim is wealth given by God and enjoyed. Ve-hishilto le'ekhol mimmennu u-lasehet helqo — 'and enables him to enjoy these things — to accept his portion.' The verb shalat (to have power over/control) is used of dominion (cf. Ecclesiastes 2:19): here God grants the person power over their own wealth to enjoy it. Ki hu mattanat Elohim hi — 'that is a gift of God.' The word mattat (H4991, gift) — from natan (to give): the capacity for enjoyment is divinely donated, the direct opposite of anxiety-driven accumulation.
20: Ki lo harbeh yizkor et-yemei chayyav — 'Such a person will not brood much over the years of their life.' Yizkor (H2142, to remember/call to mind) — the person who enjoys their portion does not obsessively rehearse the past or calculate the future. Ki ha'Elohim ma'aneh be-simhat libbo — 'because God keeps their heart occupied with joy.' The hapax ma'aneh (H6039, preoccupation/the thing that occupies/answers) derives from anah (to answer/respond/be occupied with). God does not merely grant joy as an occasional gift; he fills the person's heart with it as an ongoing occupation. The word's root meaning (to answer/respond) suggests that joy is God's ongoing answer to the question of meaning that the hevel-saturated world poses.
